data.title: Walmart: Navigating A Bumpy Tariff Environment
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Walmart (WMT) is one of the largest omnichannel retailers striving to integrate their eCommerce and retail stores. Everyday Low Prices is their philosophy, and they have earned the trust of their customers by providing a broad assortment of quality merchandise and services at low prices.

Tariffs have been a hot topic in the past couple of years. The recent U.S. Supreme Court ruling against tariffs, and the Trump administration's response, brought new layers of uncertainty and confusion to an already complicated situation.

Walmart imports much of their merchandise from other countries, and they also manufacture some of their goods on foreign soil. Therefore, they have a large exposure to tariffs, and management discussed some challenges related to tariffs in their earnings call.

In this article, I will share my analysis of the impact that tariffs have on Walmart's business and implications for the stock price.

What is going on with Tariffs?

Ever since the Trump administration announced a slate of tariffs on countries with high trade deficits with the United States on so-called Liberation Day, the global economy has been dealing with various impacts.

Some, but not all, countries made trade deals with the U.S. as a response to the tariffs.

A group of U.S. businesses filed a lawsuit against the tariffs, claiming their businesses had been severely harmed by the tariffs. Many U.S. retail and restaurant trade groups expressed concerns over the negative economic impact of the tariffs. Concerns over the tariffs have been the big story around global trade since Liberation Day.

Recently, the tension related to the tariffs increased sharply when the U.S. Supreme Court ruled that the President doesn't have the authority to impose tariffs. President Trump responded to the Supreme Court's decision by signing an executive order that would impose a 10% tariff on all countries.

In calendar year 2025, custom duties raised $264 B for the U.S. government, which was a large increase from $79 B in 2024. With the recent Supreme Court's decision, the revenue collected will have to be refunded.

One of the main stated goals of imposing tariffs was to reduce the U.S. trade deficit; however, the data shows that the trade deficit fell by just $2.1 B in 2025 compared to 2024. The decrease in the trade deficit was mostly due to the increase in the trade surplus of services, and the goods deficit actually increased by $25.5 B in 2025 compared to 2024.

Walmart's Exposure to Tariffs

So how does all this apply to Walmart?

As a big retailer that sells a large amount of imported goods, Walmart has a substantial exposure to the tariffs. The most direct impact is tariff-related cost pressure, and Walmart has been working with their merchants to keep their prices as low as possible.

Another dynamic that Walmart has to deal with is customers and members' spending behavior changes. Management reported that they saw an adjustment in spending behavior in middle- and lower-income households more so than with higher-income households. Walmart has been closely monitoring customer demand and managing quantity decisions related to the impacted items.

In the 3Q 2025 earnings call, management mentioned that they were satisfied with how they have been managing inventory, closing price gaps, and improving product mix to handle the tariffs. Since Liberation Day in 2025, Walmart has been consistent with their strategy in navigating the tariff environment. They have been playing offense by reinforcing their customers and members value propositions, improving their business models, and delivering on their financial performance.

I agree that Walmart has been doing a great job at navigating the confusing tariff and trade environment and consistently delivering value to their shareholders.

2026 Outlook

The U.S. producer prices data released in late February showed that the Producer Price Index increased by 0.5% in January (0.7%, if excluding food and energy), and that is the highest increase in more than three and a half years.

Many businesses have decided to pass on import tariffs and raised their prices at the start of 2026.

The Produce Price Index is a leading indicator of the consumer price index, and economists are expecting sticky inflation to stay in 2026.

The U.S. economic growth slowed in late 2025, and the trend will likely continue in 2026. The GDP growth in 4Q 2025 was 1.4%, a sharp decrease from 4.4% in 3Q 2025. The federal government shutdown played a role in the slowdown, and also personal spending on goods fell for the first time since 1Q 2024.

The growth profile signaled further challenges will be coming in early 2026.

High inflation, a slowing economy, and a shaky tariff environment indicate that consumers will have to tighten their budget in 2026. As Walmart is positioned as a value retailer, the recessionary environment may benefit their business. Walmart's Everyday Low Prices strategy gathers momentum and attracts customers when times are tough, compared to premium brand stores like Target.

During the Great Recession period (2007-2009), Walmart's revenue grew 10%, 9%, and 7% in 2007, 2008, and 2009, respectively. Some went so far as to claim that Walmart was an antidote to America's affordability crisis, reporting that a growing number of Americans have been heading to Walmart for groceries and clothing.

Walmart's strategy to use its massive size and scale to drive down prices and improve its logistics network for online shopping will continue to serve the American people well.

Risk

Geopolitical tensions are running high since the Trump administration came in, and the recent attack on Iran by the U.S. and Israel only increased these tensions. The military conflict in the Middle East will increase the oil price, which will push inflation up further via the high energy price. Consumer sentiment is already negative, but it will sour even more and negatively impact consumer spending. Walmart's sales may experience complications from the souring consumer sentiment.

Conclusion

The U.S. economy has been slowing down recently, and the downtrend may continue for the remainder of 2026. Consumer spending has been slowing, and the sticky inflation situation will further sour consumer sentiment in the near future.

Even after the Supreme Court's ruling, the global tariff situation is showing no signs of clarity. The bumpy tariff environment will continue for the U.S. consumers in the foreseeable future.

I believe this negative economic sentiment and uncertain tariff environment will benefit Walmart. As a value retailer, Walmart tends to gain market share when times are tough. Also, the management and employees of Walmart have been doing a great job at navigating the uncertain tariff environment and keeping their costs low for customers. I expect this trend to continue in the future.

It is never a great time to go through a slow economic cycle, but the shareholders of Walmart may come out as a rare winner of this cycle.

data.title: Millicom: Overvaluation Is Expanding Into 2026
data.description:
In this article, I'll cover one of my previously favorite telecom companies, Millicom International Cellular (TIGO). The company and my investment in it have been a bit of a "success story". I've been able to generate outperformance, quite a significant one, by investing when the valuation was inexpensive. For the past two articles, starting in late 2025, my rating has been negative/neutral. If you follow my work, you know it takes a while for me to change my ratings, and I don't change my PTs or FVs based on momentary or short-term exuberance.

In this article, I'll be addressing whether exuberance is what we have here, or if there's something more to it - if we perhaps have a trend reversal that would justify a higher valuation. It's suitable, given that we have 4Q25 results that are less than 2 weeks old.

At the same time, I want to make it clear that Millicom has seen increasing volatility for the past few years. It's changing its operations and divesting some countries entirely. In my last article, found here, I started addressing some of these issues and risks that I saw.

For the past 2 years, Millicom has undergone major strategic changes that have impacted both the company's financials and the expected returns. When I started covering it, the company was high-investment CapEx and low cash flow in terms of returns. It was a company that had yet to convince the market. Now, some of that convincing is being done, and some has already been done - and there are a few ownership changes that merit special attention and that I perhaps did not cover expansively enough in my previous article - so I'll do that here.

But Millicom started meeting my targets between 2023 and 2024, where it significantly reduced its operating costs and is still targeting close to $1B FCF by 2026.

Let's see how this is going by the end of fiscal 2025.

Upside from high returns

So, I promised some high-level and ownership news. First, the French billionaire Xavier Niel became a major shareholder in 2025. He had been an active owner before, but by 2025, he increased his share of ownership in the company to 40%, making him by far the largest and main shareholder. The delisting of the Stockholm-listed share, which, by the way, was the one that I invested in for years, was a step in this process as well. The company shifted its leadership to a new strategy - the Project Everest strategy, which I spoke to in my last article. In 4Q25, we have the first full-year results to see how this strategy has been working out.

And the first indications are very positive. Significant margin expansion in 2025 in key markets, with some of these main markets easily above 50% EBITDA margins, while others are not bad either. As a point of comparison from Europe and the USA, it's typically considered solid for a telco to have above 30-35% EBITDA, with a home market advantage of close to 39-40% - see Orange (ORANY) for examples. The fact that TIGO operates so far above this and not in one market, but several, makes this company very attractive indeed.

The second thing we're seeing is a significant reduction in operating leverage - it's down from over 3x to less than 2.4x, which has allowed TIGO to reintroduce what was once, and now still is, an attractive dividend payout.

One of the major challenges on the operational side, aside from its optimization of assets and portfolio, is the company trying to migrate prepaid to postpaid customers. Due to market characteristics in these regions, many of its clients are operating their cell phone plans, or cell phone communication, on a prepaid basis in a way that I was used to doing over 15 years ago here in Europe. The company has attempted to shift this, but it's important to point out that this prepaid model remains by far the most dominant model - numbers are unclear, but some suggest over 55-60% of the total in Brazil - so it's not unreasonable to assume similar numbers in closer countries. This is a largely untapped market, and evaluating the potential of this is one of the trickier parts of looking at LATAM here. There are well over 450M subscribers in LATAM - if Millicom was able to shift most of its customers to postpay, that's obviously a massive increase in revenue on a per customer basis.

The fact that this shift is going slowly is also suggested by the 4Q25 results - 2m postpay net adds, which is impressive, but the company's subscriber base is still mostly prepay.

The good part here was a significant FCF beat significant service revenue increases on a quarter-by-quarter basis. While the company is, to tie back to this, seeing some success in seeing its customer growth be more towards the postpay than prepay, the prepay is still the vast majority of accounts we're seeing.

There are no clear issues in the revenue, in the customer base, or in EBITDA for any of the company's geographies, really. The integration of Chile and Colombia is doing well, which will add further revenues to the company's bottom line. You might have read my, mildly put, dissatisfied report on Spanish Telefonica (TEF) - well, Millicom is one of the companies that saw a distinct advantage from the way that Telefonica was handling much of its business. The only country that can be argued to be performing poorly at this time is Bolivia - but this country is less than 8% of the total business for the company, which makes the revenue and EBITDA drop less interesting than if it had happened to something like Guatemala or Paraguay.

The company expects, due to heavy investing activity, its leverage to actually be up somewhat by year's end, while FCF is set to increase. That being said, debt maturities are not troubling. The company has a well-laddered cycle, and given current trends and a solid credit rating for what the company represents, I don't foresee any fundamental issues for the company when it refinances that debt.

No, to find risks we have to look beyond the operational performance, which, by the way, was better than I originally expected. It justifies a bit of a PT and FV increase, which I will do here going forward. I will discuss risks in detail more below - but the challenges that I see are integration-related, such as Telefonica's Chile operations. A cynical investor might say that there's a reason Telefonica sold these operations, and Colombia operations, while currently seeming to work out fine, are also something to keep an eye on.

When I started covering Millicom, the company had a stronger competitive position than it has now. New competitors have moved in, including but not limited to America Movil, Nubicom SRl, and others - some with their own networks, which is likely to put downward pressure on the ARPU.

But it's the combination of high capital intensity coupled with poor interest payment coverage that leads me to be more conservative than the average investor here.

Valuation

The coming few years of high CapEx coupled with significant interest payments, which are expected to drive up leverage, not continue to push it down, are what create an inversion of valuation here, as I see it. The company, by the way, confirms this leverage direction, citing a 2.5x net leverage in 2026, which would mark an increase from the 2.3x level we're seeing for this year.

It seems very clear that AEPS is set to decline for the year (2026), after a superb 2025. The reason is that 2025 was affected by a number of one-time, non-recurring effects. This is, for one, the significant sales proceeds from the sale of Lati to SBA, bringing in almost a billion, as well as gains related to favorable tax effects, a one-time thing cited by the company in 4Q25.

Due to this, the near-term forecast is negative, and confirmed by analysts here.

As you can see here, the combination of high CapEx and interest costs, coupled with operational pushes, is likely to deliver lower results in 2026. I'll be on the lookout for confirmation of this in 1Q26 and 2Q26, for confirmation in my thesis. While I will be increasing my overall targets for the company, I will also be stating very clearly that I view this as an opportunity to rotate. The rotation potential, no matter where you really entered, is significant. I do not see a good argument to stay in this 4-5% yielding telco investment, given what it is going into from an operational and strategic perspective here. Confirming this, several analysts have already downgraded TIGO to "underperform", including Scotiabank. I believe the company is a very good example of a surge, a rally that was justified based on how well the company has been doing, but that is no longer justified here.

My fair value for TIGO is $50/share. I'm increasing it to $55/share. As weird as this may sound, it's not a substantial change in my expectations. My FV of $55/share, and now PT of $43/share, still represents a significant correction and is the value that many analysts cite as something close to "fair value" for the company. It represents a forward P/E of 13-14x, and an EV/EBITDA, again on a forward basis, of 5x. More importantly, this aligns with the average of LATAM telcos, below typical European and US bases.

In this estimate, I am assuming integration challenges not only for Colombia but also for Chile. Given the market dynamics in LATAM and coupling this with the company's debt payment and interest payment trends, I do not view this as an unrealistic risk or expectation.

Note that this company did trade below $17/share no more than 2 years ago. That's the position I went into at the time. I bought it very cheaply. My price target and FV might seem odd from today's perspective. However, I have learned that this is the bane of the valuation-oriented investor. Whenever we go in, our price target seems excessively high because we're pricing for value. Exactly the same is true when the company has bounced significantly - our price targets are then viewed, once again, as way too conservative because the company is "obviously better".

I maintain a steady course and remain, as always, unbothered by external views of my price targets and assumptions, provided that they make sense to me. They do in this case. The increase in PT and FV represents a higher earnings potential as well as a growing rate of Postpaid from Prepaid, which I view as valid.

For risks in 2026, I see the following.

Risks & Considerations

Additions from Colombia and Ecuador will offset that top-line drop and even exceed it, but I don't see where it would justify this company trading at what amounts to a 200-300% premium to European or NA telcos, which is what the current estimates and assumptions seem to be doing. I would view this as exuberance, and I do.

The one significant risk to growth, in my view, and the source of the uncertainty for this company, is the emerging markets in which it operates. Specifically, these markets are highly dependent on US remittances and trade for large portions of their annual national revenues, and cuts in that, which is not unlikely given today's politically-charged climate, could easily spill over into other industries. The way that US policy is moving these days, however, I don't view that as being all that likely.

My updated thesis for 2026 is as follows.

Thesis

Millicom is a company in the telecommunications business that is the equivalent of a growth stock in this sector, but one that I believe is very likely to underperform, in the near term, not outperform. The company has, however, seen significant returns, which in 2025 warranted a profit rotation for me. I am now, as of 2026, happily out of this investment with very solid returns. The risk/reward ratio is also significant here compared to what's otherwise available on the market and what qualities telcos are known for.

On the back of a solid Q4'25 performance and future potential, I give TIGO a conservative PT of $43/share, but based on current levels, consider it a "Hold" at this time. My fair value target for the company comes to $55/share, but this is definitely as high as I am willing to go.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, a valuation target of $38/share is where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of March 2026 after Q4'25.

data.title: Anthropic Isn't Overthrowing Software, It Might Just Be Rewiring It
data.description:
One of Wall Street's favorite sectors just hit the reset button after a significant update to Anthropic's "CoWork" platform. In recent weeks, according to Fortune, the U.S. software sector experienced a widespread sell-off, losing over $1 trillion in market value, after news broke about Anthropic's new agentic artificial intelligence (AI) capabilities.1

Now, investors are grappling with an important question: Could advances in AI, including those from Anthropic, fundamentally reshape how businesses use software?

We believe the concern is not purely theoretical. Anthropic’s recent advancements in its AI systems and agentic workflow automation seem to have captured the attention of both enterprise leaders and Wall Street, fueling speculation that companies may one day rely less on traditional software subscriptions and more on intelligent agents capable of executing tasks autonomously.

How Does Anthropic’s AI Work?

Anthropic’s vision centers on AI agents that function less like tools and more like digital colleagues. Rather than navigating multiple applications, users can delegate complex workflows to an AI system that operates across various platforms.

The concept behind systems sometimes described as “co-worker” agents or autonomous task bots is straightforward but powerful: combine large language models (LLMs) with memory, reasoning capabilities, and secure integrations into enterprise environments.

LLM function calling plays a key role here. It allows the model to trigger application programming interfaces (APIs), software interfaces, or internal tools on the user’s behalf, which is what makes this kind of AI feel more like a coworker than an advisor. The agent can interpret goals, break them into steps, use these software interfaces or APIs to actually execute tasks, and adapt as conditions change.

Will Human User Interface Transition To Agent Interface?

The idea that AI, like Anthropic-style “co‑workers” and agentic workflows, will kill software is overstated. We believe AI is changing who operates software, not whether software is needed at all.

Generative and agentic AI could shift the primary operator of software from people to digital agents. Instead of users clicking through applications, they describe goals to AI agents, which execute workflows across tools, APIs, and data sources. Anthropic’s “AI co-worker” model illustrates this: an agent can pull customer relationship management (CRM) data, analyze trends, draft slides, and route approvals in minutes, compressing multi-hour workflows into a single instruction.

In this example, software does not disappear. It becomes the execution substrate that agents orchestrate in the background, like the systems of record (the authoritative systems where core business data lives), permissions, and processes that AI relies on to act safely and accurately.

Systems Of Record: AI’s Critical Context Layer

Enterprise software’s real moat was never the user interface (UI). It is the central data model, workflow engine, permission boundary, and audit trail that keep organizations aligned and compliant.

Examples include how Microsoft (MSFT) positions Copilot as useful because it plugs into Microsoft 365 data and Microsoft Graph while preserving security and compliance boundaries. Salesforce’s agent capabilities ground outputs in structured CRM objects like Accounts, Contacts, and Cases. Palantir’s (PLTR) Ontology organizes an enterprise’s digital and physical assets into a graph that agents can understand and act on.

When people say “we do not need software anymore,” they usually mean we do not need so many UIs. AI does not eliminate systems of record; it increases the premium on accurate, governed, well-structured ones.

Why Software as a Service Isn’t Going Away

AI-native workflows have contributed to heightened investor uncertainty around Software as a Service (SaaS) stocks. Valuations that once assumed AI would simply lift productivity are being reassessed by some investors as enterprises experiment with custom agents and automation. Some narrow, tool-like applications that only provide a thin UI over simple tasks could be at risk if an agent can produce the same outcome without a separate interface.

But core platforms, like CRM, enterprise resource planning (ERP), human resources information systems (HRIS), information technology service management (ITSM), and collaboration hubs, are deeply embedded in how organizations operate. Replacing them is constrained by integration dependencies, process redesign, training, and change management, often on multi-year timelines.

Panorama Consulting Group’s 2024 ERP report stresses that AI projects succeed only when tightly integrated into existing systems and supported by robust business process management (BPM) guidance. Research from Gartner, cited by multiple industry outlets, forecasts that more than 40% of agentic AI projects will be canceled by 2027 due to rising costs and unclear business value, underscoring how hard it is to get production deployments right.2

The bottleneck is not better technology in the abstract; it is production reliability, governance, and organizational change. Those are the domains where established SaaS systems have tended to be more difficult to displace.

Production Reality: “API Works” Does Not Mean “Production Works”

Most impressive AI demos ignore real-world enterprise constraints: role-based access, data privacy, auditability, uptime, latency, and cost controls. Once those requirements appear, complexity multiplies, and off-the-shelf models are not enough. Vendors like Anthropic still need engineering teams embedded with customers to wire AI into existing systems, workflows, and compliance regimes.

Industry research shows that AI and ERP initiatives only deliver value when they are tightly integrated with current applications and guided by explicit process design. Gartner’s projection that over 40% of agentic AI efforts may be scrapped by 2027 is a warning: ungoverned AI pilots are easy; durable, return-on-investment (ROI) positive production systems are hard.2 That reality favors platforms with mature integration surfaces, security models, and ecosystems.

SaaS Moats Are Socio-Technical

SaaS is “sticky” not just because of its features, but because of the people around it. The admins, consultants, training programs, certificates, and online communities all build a powerful moat around these core platforms. Salesforce’s Trailhead learning platform, for example, has millions of “Trailblazers” earning badges and building careers that are directly tied to Salesforce. When someone’s job, reputation, and pay are built on a system, they will fight to keep that system in place.

AI may change what these jobs look like, moving work from manual setup and report writing to overseeing AI agents, cleaning data, and enforcing rules. But those changes may make the core systems of record (the main, trusted source of key data) even more important, not less.

The Real Shift: From Seats To Digital Labor

The popular story is that SaaS apps will just turn into chat windows, where you “talk” to your CRM or ERP, and it does the work for you. The more realistic transformation is not “SaaS to chat” but “seats to metered digital labor,” where you pay less for human users and more for work done by software agents, billed based on usage. Instead of only selling per-user licenses, vendors are starting to price the actual work: actions taken, events processed, or conversations handled by AI agents. Salesforce’s Agentforce, for example, already blends traditional user licenses with consumption-based Flex Credits, so customers pay per agent action and can match spend more directly to measurable outcomes. ServiceNow and other workflow platforms are moving the same way, defining and monetizing discrete AI “assists” rather than just selling more seats.

In this world, we believe the winners will be platforms that:

  • Expose clean, secure APIs and event streams for agents.
  • Treat agents as first-class users of their systems of record.
  • Monetize agent traffic with transparent, outcome-linked pricing.

Horizontal workflow platforms that own core data and governance, and that embrace an agent-first model, may be positioned to succeed.

Implications For Investors: Disruption, Not Extinction?

For public markets, AI introduces genuine volatility. Large, deeply integrated platforms must invest heavily to become agent-native and shift pricing toward digital labor. However, history suggests that innovations and platform shifts may change industry leadership rather than erase entire sectors.

Additionally, some investors may be looking beyond public equities to capture innovation in the AI value chain. That is why we launched a public-private AI ETF, the KraneShares Artificial Intelligence & Technology ETF (AGIX), a fund primarily focused on U.S. markets that provides exposure to public companies with select private innovators such as Anthropic and SpaceX (through the xAI-SpaceX merger). This kind of public–private lens can help capture both incumbents adapting software to agent substrates and frontier labs building the agents themselves.

For investors interested in China's technology industry, we launched a China AI ETF, the KraneShares CSI China Internet ETF (KWEB). KWEB provides exposure to publicly listed companies that are influencing China’s LLM, video generation, and applied AI stack, from models like Alibaba’s (BABA) Qwen and Baidu’s (BIDU) ERNIE to Tencent’s (TCEHY) Hunyuan.

Similarly, we launched an Emerging Market Technology ETF, the KraneShares Emerging Markets Consumer Technology ETF (KEMQ). KEMQ extends this AI and technology theme beyond China, targeting global companies that are building the digital infrastructure and AI solutions serving billions of emerging-market consumers, such as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC)(TSM) and South Korea's SK Hynix (HXSC.F).

Citations:

  1. Data from "Anthropic’s Claude triggered a trillion-dollar selloff. A new upgrade could make things worse," Fortune, 2/6/2026.
  2. Data from “Over 40% of agentic AI projects will be scrapped by 2027, Gartner says,” Reuters, 6/25/2025.

Definitions:

Large language models (LLMs): An artificial intelligence system trained on vast amounts of text to learn patterns in human language so it can understand prompts and generate human-like responses.

User interface (UI): The screens, buttons, and menus that people interact with when they use software.

Application programming interface (API): A set of rules that allows different software programs to communicate and share data or functions with each other.

Customer relationship management (CRM): Software that helps companies track and manage interactions with customers and sales prospects.

Enterprise resource planning (ERP): Software that integrates key business functions such as finance, supply chain, manufacturing, and procurement into one system.

Human resources information system (HRIS): Software that manages employee-related data, including payroll, benefits, performance, and hiring.

Information technology service management (ITSM): Software and processes that help IT teams deliver, manage, and support technology services across an organization.

Software as a Service (SaaS): A software delivery model where customers access applications over the internet and pay a recurring subscription rather than buying and installing the software themselves.

Business process management (BPM): The practice of analyzing, designing, and improving business workflows to make them more efficient and effective.

Return on investment (ROI): A measure of how much financial benefit a project or product generates compared with its cost.

Salesforce Trailblazers: In Salesforce’s ecosystem, “Trailblazers” are the pioneers and lifelong learners who use Salesforce to innovate, build their careers, companies, and communities, and help others succeed with the platform.

Token as a Service (TaaS): A service model where a provider manages the creation, issuance, and lifecycle of digital tokens (often for blockchain, access, or usage-based billing) so clients can use tokens without building their own infrastructure.

Small and medium‑sized businesses (SMBs): Commercial organizations that fall below a country or industry’s thresholds for large enterprises, typically defined by employee count and/or annual revenue.

data.title: Nomura Tax-Free USA Intermediate Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

The US municipal bond market recorded positive returns for 4Q25, driven primarily by strong performance in October. The US economy showed signs of slowing, as it had in previous quarters, resulting in monetary easing. Additionally, political uncertainty and fiscal policy influenced the market. The municipal market contended with a technical headwind of heavy issuance, which was a theme all year. Demand was strong, however, as municipal mutual funds experienced inflows. The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index returned 1.56%. The intermediate segment of the yield curve outperformed while the investment grade segment outperformed high yield segments.

Increased trade tensions between the US and China, giving way to a truce, marked the beginning of the last quarter of 2025. Economic indicators continued to show resilience with 3Q gross domestic product ('GDP') growth tracking close to 3.5%, maintaining the pace of 2Q (based on the Atlanta Federal Reserve's GDPNow forecast). Government fiscal deficits and artificial intelligence ('AI') spending remained tailwinds. Investors mostly shrugged off the US government shutdown during the quarter, viewing it as unlikely to significantly impact growth. The lack of important data releases was the larger concern. Labor markets showed some softness with the unemployment rate rising to 4.6%, while inflation appears to be moderating with November's Consumer Price Index ('CPI') reported at 2.7% versus an expected 3.1%. However, the impact of the government shutdown on data quality was visible with some parts of the CPI report in particular reflecting missing October data. The reported disinflation, while welcome, will need to hold up in coming months before the US Federal Reserve (Fed) and investors are convinced that further easing is likely.

The economy and the markets were also supported by policy stimulus – deficit spending and Fed easing. The fiscal impulse is widely expected to continue in 2026 with tax refunds under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act ('OBBBA') flowing through in the first quarter. The Fed resumed its easing in September, but cracks are visible within the committee with some members concerned about the downside risks to labor markets while others fret about stimulating even higher inflation. While less-restrictive monetary policy as a risk-management exercise appeared to be a reasonable compromise, investors are increasingly worried that further easing may signal a weakening of the Fed's resolve on inflation.

The Treasury yield curve steepened as yields on the front end of the curve fell while rising on the long segment during the quarter. For example, the 2-year Treasury yield fell 14 basis points (bps), while the 10-year spot remained unchanged and the 30-year segment rose 9 bps. (One basis point equals one-hundredth of a percentage point.) Municipal yields rose on the front end, fell in the intermediate segments, and remained unchanged on the long end. The AAA municipal 2-year yield rose 9 bps, the 10-year fell 16 bps, and the 30-year was flat. Consequently, municipal bonds underperformed Treasury bonds on the front end while outperforming in the intermediate and long segments of the curve. MMD AAA/US Treasury tightened roughly 170-390 bps in the 10- and 30-year segments of the curve. Despite the strong ratio movement, the 10-year tranche ended the year at roughly the same level that it started, and the 30-year tranche was wider than where it began the year. The municipal curve, as measured by the AAA 2-year minus the AAA 30-year, flattened 9 bps for the quarter but still was 77 bps steeper for the full year. It finished at 185 bps after being as steep as 243 bps in August.

Municipal mutual funds recorded inflows of $19.5 billion for the quarter. Open-end funds accounted for $6.6 billion while exchange-traded funds (ETFs) registered $12.9 billion in inflows. Investment grade funds took in roughly 88% of the inflows. From a duration perspective, long-term (10+ years) funds took in roughly $8.3 billion. More specifically, long ETFs took in most of that amount. In the last few weeks of 2025, year-end tax-loss swapping from open-end funds into ETFs occurred. For example, open-end funds experienced roughly -$1.3 billion in outflows while ETFs took in roughly $2.8 billion in inflows during the last three weeks of the year. For the full calendar year, municipal mutual funds experienced $51.0 billion in inflows with ETFs accounting for roughly 67% of that amount.

Issuance for the quarter was robust, a continual trend all year as issuers attempted to lock in borrowing costs amid fiscal policy uncertainty. More than $144 billion in new-issue supply came to the market, 15% higher than 4Q24. There were more than a dozen deals over $1.0 billion in size. For the full calendar year, the municipal bond market issuance registered a record, totaling more than $590 billion. As stated previously, the uptick could be attributed to a “pull forward” effect in preparation for potential fiscal policy limitations as well as the suppressed need for capital and the impact of inflation on the market’s funding needs.

Against this backdrop, the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index returned 1.56% for the quarter, bolstered by strong performance in the first month. The index’s October return of 1.24% was the first positive October return in the past six years and well above the 20-year monthly average of -0.31%. The index returned 4.25% for the full year, above the 20-year annual average of 3.60%. The 10-year segment of the curve was the strongest performer while the long end (20+ years) was the weakest for the year, an aberration from long-term curve trends.

The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index outperformed other major US bond benchmarks for 4Q25 (see returns table below). The strongest-performing part of the municipal curve was the 15-year (12-17 years), which returned 2.84%. The 3-year segment (2-4 years) was the weakest, returning 0.46%. The strongest-performing investment grade credit tranche was BBB-rated bonds, returning 1.77%, while the A-rated segment was the weakest, returning 1.50%. The Bloomberg High-Yield Municipal Bond Index returned 1.15% for the quarter, modestly trailing the Bloomberg US Corporate High-Yield Index’s 1.31% gain. Similar to the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, the Bloomberg High Yield Index’s 12-17 year segment of the yield curve outperformed. High yield tobacco and high yield Puerto Rico bonds, which together make up roughly 21% of the Bloomberg High-Yield Municipal Bond Index, returned -1.75% and 1.12%, respectively.

Market statistics

During 4Q25, US Treasury yields fell by 4 bps in the 5-year segment, remained flat in the 10-year, and rose 9 bps in the 30-year. The municipal bond yield rose 9 bps in the 5-year segment, fell 16 bps in the 10-year, and remained flat in the 30-year.

As shown in the following table, MMD AAA/US Treasury ratios were mixed with outperformance on the front end and underperformance on the long end for the quarter.

The municipal yield curve, as measured by the spread between 2-year and 30-year MMD AAA-rated bond yields, flattened by 9 bps, to 185 bps. Two-year high-grade tax-exempt bond yields rose by 9 bps, while 30-year high-grade tax-exempt bond yields were unchanged. (Data: Thomson Reuters.)

BBB-rated securities led the investment grade municipal market during 4Q25, returning 1.77%. Credit spreads, as measured by MMD yields on 30-year BBB-rated general obligation bonds minus 30-year AAA-rated general obligation bonds, were 3 bps tighter. At the end of the quarter, the spread was 87 bps.

Municipal bond total returns by rating for the quarter were as follows:

The housing sector returned 1.77% and was the strongest sector within the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index. The weakest sector was industrial redevelopment/pollution control revenue (IDR/PCR) bonds, which returned 0.65%.

Municipal supply was approximately $144.0 billion for 4Q25, a 15% increase relative to 4Q24. (Sources: Bloomberg Finance LP, J.P. Morgan.)

Within the Fund

The Fund's full allocation to out-of-benchmark below investment grade bonds detracted from performance, returning -1.68% compared to the benchmark return of 1.48% and the Bloomberg High Yield Municipal Bond Index return of 1.15%. From a curve perspective, the Fund's roughly 45.0% exposure to out-of-benchmark long bonds (17+ years) detracted. These bonds returned 1.26% compared to the benchmark return of 1.48%. For reference, the 17+ year allocation within the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index returned 1.85%.

The 15-year (12-17 years) maturity segment contributed for the quarter. This was the strongest-performing maturity segment in the benchmark, returning 2.82%. The Fund was market weight and outperformed in this part of the curve, returning 2.90%.

Outlook

The municipal market delivered strong returns in 2025. The short/intermediate portion of the curve drove performance while the long end lagged, an aberration from long-term norms. However, the municipal curve did begin to normalize toward the back half of the year. For example, the MMD AAA 2-year/30-year curve was as steep as 242 bps but finished the year at 185 bps. We believe the curve can continue to flatten as the 10-year average is 130 bps.

After record issuance in 2025, we believe next year's new issue volume will be similarly elevated with sufficient demand to offset the issuance. Fund flows were positive in 2025. These flows were primarily directed toward long (10+ year) ETFs and intermediate (5-10 year) funds, while long-term (10+ year) open end funds have yet to receive significant inflows. We believe there is still significant money parked in short-term vehicles waiting to be deployed. If the Fed continues its easing program, we think the inflow momentum should continue. In addition to organic fund flows, calls and coupon interest, which hit record highs in 2025, are projected to be even higher in 2026 and should be a supportive technical.

As we noted in previous quarters, we believe rates will be range-bound but ultimately lower by the end of 2026. However, we expect periods of volatility, which we would view as an opportunity to add to allocations. In our view, municipal credit remains fundamentally strong, bolstered by solid economic performance. Additionally, we generally do not anticipate a significant degradation in municipal credit. Yields in the municipal market offer investors the opportunity to receive healthy tax-free income, accompanied by attractive total return potential. We recommend investors consider using volatility to add to their municipal exposures.

Market risk is the risk that all or a majority of the securities in a certain market – like the stock market or bond market – will decline in value because of factors such as adverse political or economic conditions, future expectations, investor confidence, or heavy institutional selling. • Fixed income securities can lose value, including the possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities are subject to credit risk and interest rate risk. Credit risk is the risk that an issuer of a fixed income security may be unable to make interest payments and/or repay principal in a timely manner. Interest rate risk is the risk that prices of bonds and other fixed income securities will increase as interest rates fall and decrease as interest rates rise. Fixed income securities with longer maturities or duration generally are more sensitive to interest rate changes. • High yield securities ("junk bonds") are subject to reduced creditworthiness of issuers, increased risk of default, and a more limited and less liquid secondary market. High yield securities may also be subject to greater price volatility and risk of loss of income and principal than higher-rated securities. • If a bond issuer prepays a bond during a period of low interest rates, the fund may have to reinvest that money at lower interest rates. • The fund or strategy is subject to liquidity risk, which is the risk that the fund or strategy's investments cannot be readily sold within seven calendar days at approximately the price at which the fund or strategy has valued them. • If a fund or strategy invests in bonds whose income is subject to the alternative minimum tax, that portion of the fund or strategy's distributions would be taxable for investors who are subject to this tax. • Governments or regulatory authorities may take actions that could adversely affect various sectors of the securities markets and affect performance. • Heightened sensitivity to regional, state, US territories or possessions, and local political and economic conditions could adversely affect performance. • The value of securities in a particular industry or sector will decline because of changing expectations for the performance of that industry or sector.

The Bloomberg 3-15 Year Blend Municipal Bond Index measures the total return performance of investment grade, US tax-exempt bonds with maturities from 2 to 17 years. The Lipper Intermediate Municipal Debt Funds Average compares funds that invest in municipal debt issues with dollar-weighted average maturities of 5 to 10 years. The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index measures the total return performance of the long-term investment grade tax-exempt bond market. The Bloomberg High-Yield Municipal Bond Index measures the total return performance of the long-term, non-investment-grade tax-exempt bond market. The Bloomberg US Aggregate Index is a broad-based benchmark that measures the investment grade, US dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. The Bloomberg US Treasury Index measures the performance of US Treasury bonds and notes that have at least one year to maturity. The Bloomberg US Corporate High-Yield Index is composed of US dollar-denominated, non-investment-grade corporate bonds for which the middle rating among Moody's Investors Service, Inc., Fitch, Inc., and Standard & Poor's is Ba1/BB+/BB+ or below. The Dow Jones Industrial Average is an often-quoted market indicator that comprises 30 widely held US blue-chip stocks. The S&P 500 Index measures the performance of 500 mostly large-cap stocks weighted by market value and is often used to represent performance of the US stock market. The US Consumer Price Index ('CPI') is a measure of inflation that is calculated by the US Department of Labor, representing changes in prices of all goods and services purchased for consumption by urban households.

The yield curve is a line that plots the interest rates, at a set point in time, of bonds having equal credit quality, but differing maturity dates. The most frequently reported yield curve compares the 3-month, 2-year, 5-year, and 30-year US Treasury debt. This yield curve is used as a benchmark for other debt in the market, such as mortgage rates or bank lending rates. It is also used to predict changes in economic output and growth. The shape of the yield curve is closely scrutinized because it helps to give an idea of future interest rate change and economic activity. There are three main types of yield curve shapes: normal, inverted and flat (or humped). A normal yield curve is one in which longer maturity bonds have a higher yield compared to shorter-term bonds due to the risks associated with time. An inverted yield curve is one in which the shorter-term yields are higher than the longer-term yields, which can be a sign of upcoming recession. A flat (or humped) yield curve is one in which the shorter- and longer-term yields are very close to each other, which is also a predictor of an economic transition. The slope of the yield curve is also seen as important: the greater the slope, the greater the gap between short- and long-term rates. Gross domestic product is a measure of all goods and services produced by a nation in a year. It is a measure of economic activity.
